narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 74.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection to protect the university's outcomes and reputation. This decision hinged primarily on your academic performance in school, particularly your low rankings relative to peers. Your third-year decile rank of 1—far below the typical student's midpoint around 5.5—stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model that your later academic trajectory raised serious concerns about bar readiness. Next in influence was your first-year decile rank, also at the bottom of the scale compared to the average around 5.7, reinforcing a pattern of underperformance from the start. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 34 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing since it's reasonably competitive, though not enough to offset the other issues. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, slightly below the average of 3.21, and middle family income bracket played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers here. Like most applicants, your plan to attend full-time aligned with the norm and didn't sway the outcome much. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your early and sustained low decile rankings deviate sharply from their stronger academic showings, which is why the model flagged higher risk in your profile. While this rejection stings, especially given your background and dedication to full-time study, it highlights clear areas for growth. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile ranks, preparing rigorously for a higher LSAT retake, and building a stronger GPA through targeted academic support. Many students in similar positions have rebounded by addressing these gaps—reach out to advisors for personalized strategies, and reapply once you've strengthened your record. Your path to law school isn't closed; it's just calling for these focused improvements.
